did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning algorithms and ensembles (including Random Forest, SVM, AD Tree) with majority voting to identify melanoma skin cancer from dermoscopy images. Features were extracted using Haar Wavelet Pyramid transforms from the ISIC dataset, and the models were trained and evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation to classify images as benign or malignant.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a health informatics system for melanoma identification. It details a specific methodology involving Haar wavelet pyramid feature extraction and training of machine learning algorithms (Random Forest, SVM, AD Tree) and ensembles on the ISIC dataset. The study includes an experimentation environment, 10-fold cross-validation, and reports original results regarding accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, distinguishing it from a review or survey.

title: Haar Wavelet Pyramid-Based Melanoma Skin Cancer Identification With Ensemble of Machine Learning Algorithms
journal: International Journal of Healthcare Information Systems and Informatics
brief_summary: This study proposes a computer-assisted diagnostic system for identifying melanoma skin cancer using dermoscopy images from the ISIC dataset. The methodology involves extracting features via the Haar Wavelet Pyramid transform at various levels and training machine learning algorithms, including Random Forest, SVM, and AD Tree, as well as ensembles using majority voting. The models were evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation, with performance measured by accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. Results indicated that performance improved as the Haar pyramid level increased from 4x4 to 64x64, with Random Forest achieving the best overall metrics. The study concludes that ensembles of machine learning algorithms provide a performance boost compared to individual algorithms, supporting the use of such systems to alleviate the shortage of expert doctors.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on identifying melanoma skin cancer. The dataset used (ISIC) comprised 2000 images, specifically split into 1000 images of benign melanoma and 1000 images of malignant melanoma. The classification task was to detect dermoscopy images as malignant or benign.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopy images into benign melanoma or malignant melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'identify melanoma skin cancer' and 'detect the dermoscopy images as malignant or benign.' The dataset consists of 1000 benign and 1000 malignant images. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, which are standard metrics for binary classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A public collection of dermoscopy images provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. The dataset used in this study consists of 2000 JPG images with metadata supplying actual diagnoses.

use.training: Used for training machine learning algorithms (Random Forest, SVM, AD Tree) and ensembles via 10-fold cross-validation.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Average of accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure uniform stratification of data and evaluate the performance of machine learning algorithms and ensembles.
To measure the proportion of the sum of positives and negatives accurately recognized.
To measure the proportion of positives that the algorithm recognizes accurately.
To measure the proportion of negatives which are accurately recognized.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity via 10-fold cross-validation, with comparisons made based on observed metric values rather than statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive.dataset_description: 2000 dermoscopy images (1000 benign melanoma, 1000 malignant melanoma)
metrics: Average of accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity

Random Forest.accuracy: Best overall accuracy observed at Haar Pyramid level 64x64 (Level 3)
Random Forest.sensitivity: Not explicitly quantified as a single best value, but noted as part of the leading performance in ensemble comparisons
Random Forest.specificity: Better specificity observed at Haar Pyramid level 32x32 (Level 4)
Random Forest.average_metric: Leading performance at Haar Pyramid level 64x64 (Level 3)

ISIC Archive.notes: Performance gradually improves from Haar wavelet pyramid level 4x4 to 16x16 and shows marginal improvement further. The ensemble of machine learning algorithms showed a boost in performance metrics compared to individual algorithms. Random Forest gave better melanoma identification in all performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy). Haar Pyramid levels of sizes 8x8, 16x16, 32x32, and 64x64 give approximately similar performance when considering the average of all three metrics with equal importance.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of Machine Learning Algorithms (Random Forest, Support Vector Machine (SVM), AD Tree) using Majority Voting; Individual models: Naive Bayes, Random Forest, SVM, AD Tree

training_details.software: Python, WEKA (Waikato Environment for Knowledge Analysis)

classification_or_output_head.output_mechanism: Majority voting for ensembles; probability/hyperplane decision for individual algorithms

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 512x512 pixels for uniformity
Haar Wavelet Pyramid transform for feature extraction at levels 64x64, 32x32, 16x16, 8x8, and 4x4
Discarding Haar Wavelet Pyramid levels 256x256 and 128x128 to reduce computational requirements
Processing images in RGB color space
conclusions: The proposed computer-assisted diagnostic system using Haar Wavelet Pyramid feature extraction and machine learning ensembles effectively identifies melanoma skin cancer from dermoscopy images, addressing the shortage of expert doctors.

Ensembles of machine learning algorithms (specifically SVM-AD Tree-Random Forest using majority voting) provide a performance boost in metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity compared to individual machine learning algorithms.
Random Forest achieved the best overall performance for melanoma identification across accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, particularly at the Haar Pyramid level of 64x64 (Level 3).
Performance gradually improves as the Haar wavelet pyramid level increases from 4x4 to 16x16, with only marginal improvements observed at higher levels (32x32 and 64x64).
Haar Pyramid levels of sizes 8x8, 16x16, 32x32, and 64x64 yield approximately similar performance when considering the average of accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity with equal importance.
The ensemble of SVM-AD Tree-Random Forest demonstrated better sensitivity performance specifically with the 4x4 Haar Pyramid level.
Better specificity was observed with Random Forest using the Haar Pyramid level of size 32x32 (Level 4).
Using higher levels of the Haar wavelet pyramid helps speed up the identification process while maintaining robust performance.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, highly balanced dataset of only 2000 images (1000 benign, 1000 malignant) from the ISIC archive, which limits the generalizability of the results to broader, real-world clinical populations with class imbalance.

No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely using 10-fold cross-validation on the single source dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and lack of robustness across different imaging devices or demographics.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between algorithms or Haar pyramid levels, relying instead on observed metric values.

The specific numerical values for accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity are not explicitly stated in the text for most models; results are primarily presented through comparative figures and percentage improvements relative to a baseline, making precise replication difficult.

The feature extraction method discards higher-resolution Haar Pyramid levels (256x256 and 128x128) to reduce computational load, which may result in the loss of fine-grained diagnostic features critical for melanoma detection.

The study compares traditional machine learning algorithms (Random Forest, SVM, AD Tree) against deep learning methods only in the literature review, without benchmarking the proposed method against state-of-the-art deep learning models (e.g., CNNs) on the same dataset.
The authors acknowledge that feature vector generation time is a concern, suggesting future work on hybridization, indicating potential latency issues for real-time clinical deployment.

The dataset metadata provides 'actual diagnoses' but does not detail the inter-rater reliability or the specific criteria used by the experts who labeled the ISIC images, introducing potential label noise.
